title: 3 John
author: 'The elder' — John the apostle
setting: A personal letter to Gaius, commending his hospitality to traveling missionaries, while condemning Diotrephes who refuses to receive them and maligns the elder.
purpose: To commend the faithful Gaius for supporting missionaries and to censure Diotrephes for his divisive, power-hungry behavior — providing a snapshot of conflict over authority and hospitality in the early church.
themes: Hospitality as partnership in the truth
Contrasting models: Gaius and Demetrius (good) vs. Diotrephes (bad)

before.label: 2 John — limits of hospitality; truth as the condition of welcome
1 John — love as the life of the community
John's Gospel — Jesus the true shepherd who serves rather than lords

after.label: Revelation — John's final canonical word from Patmos
The canonical Johannine corpus completed (Gospel + 3 letters)
The canon complete — faithful elders guard the apostolic deposit in every generation

key_people.name: The Elder
key_people.role: Writes a pastoral and personal letter dealing with a specific conflict between two leaders with opposing approaches to hospitality and authority

key_people.name: Gaius
key_people.role: The letter's recipient, commended for his faithfulness and hospitality to travelling brothers — the model of what Christian leadership looks like

key_people.name: Diotrephes
key_people.role: A church leader who loves to put himself first (v. 9), refuses to receive the elder's authority, and prevents others from extending hospitality to brothers — a negative case study in leadership

key_people.name: Demetrius
key_people.role: A third figure commended by everyone, by the truth itself, and by the Elder — likely the bearer of the letter, who represents the alternative to Diotrephes

context: 3 John was written c. AD 90-100, likely from Ephesus, as the shortest NT book (219 Greek words). It addresses a specific conflict between the Elder's authority and a local leader named Diotrephes who has been refusing the Elder's letters and his emissaries. The letter provides a case study in church conflict, authority, and the ethics of leadership.
christ_connection: 3 John does not cite Christ by name in most manuscripts, but its theology is fully christological. The 'name' for whose sake the travelling brothers go out (v. 7) is the name of Christ; the 'truth' in which Gaius walks (vv. 3-4) is the truth of the gospel; and the pattern of Gaius's hospitality — receiving strangers who are brothers in the faith — is an expression of the love that characterises Christ's own welcome. Diotrephes's refusal to receive the brothers and his slander of the Elder represent the antithesis of the servant-leadership that Christ modelled (Mark 10:45).

key_verses.note: 'I have no greater joy than to hear that my children are walking in the truth' — the pastoral heart of the Elder: the deepest satisfaction is the faithfulness of those he has taught
'Beloved, do not imitate evil but imitate good. Whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God' — the ethical principle that underlies the contrast between Gaius and Diotrephes

themes_detail.title: Hospitality as a Theological Practice
themes_detail.text: 3 John is built around the practice of hospitality to travelling missionaries — what Gaius exemplifies and Diotrephes refuses. The theological stakes are significant: these brothers go out 'for the sake of the name, accepting nothing from the Gentiles' (v. 7), meaning they depend entirely on Christian hospitality for their work. Those who receive them become 'fellow workers for the truth' (v. 8); those who refuse them obstruct the missionary enterprise. Hospitality in the NT context is not mere social courtesy but a form of active participation in the spread of the gospel.

themes_detail.title: Leadership, Authority, and Pride
themes_detail.text: Diotrephes is one of the few individual church leaders named and criticised in the NT. His offences are: loving to be first, not acknowledging the Elder's authority, spreading malicious nonsense, refusing to welcome brothers, and preventing those who want to do so (v. 9-10). The psychological profile — ego-driven leadership that excludes rivals and resists legitimate authority — is recognisable in every era of church history. John's response is not to denounce from a distance but to promise a personal visit (v. 10): pastoral accountability requires presence.

themes_detail.title: The Imitation of Good and the Test of Character
themes_detail.text: The letter's ethical maxim (v. 11 — imitate good, not evil; whoever does good is from God; whoever does evil has not seen God) is presented as the interpretive lens for the Gaius-Diotrephes contrast. Gaius is a model to imitate; Diotrephes is a warning to heed. This pedagogical use of positive and negative examples is characteristic of the Johannine letters and of NT ethics generally: abstract principles are always illustrated in concrete persons. Character is revealed in how one treats those who depend on one's generosity.
